notes.text: Complete the FAQ render in the article body: The third FAQ pair ('What is the FCA Mills Review and when will it affect fintech firms?') is truncated mid-answer in the published body. The frontmatter pair is fully written — ensure the formatter pulls all three pairs through to the rendered section. Two FAQs against a declared has_faq=true is a reader-facing gap and a missed featured-snippet opportunity.

Internal link path format: Both internal links use full domain URLs (https://theparticlepost.com/...) rather than the relative slug paths specified in the SEO guidelines (e.g., /posts/fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment/). Relative paths are more resilient to domain changes and are the format the cross-link map specifies. Switch to relative markdown links for all future articles.

Em dash discipline: The FINRA/NASCUS paragraph in 'What Research Actually Shows' uses two em dashes as stylistic connectors within the same paragraph. Editorial guidelines flag repeated em dash use as an AI-generation signal. Restructure one of those clauses — make it its own sentence or use a colon — so no paragraph contains more than one em dash. This is the only stylistic issue holding the article back from a clean score.

The final FAQ answer is truncated mid-sentence ('The programme runs'). This appears to be a copy/paste artifact from the draft pipeline. Before publication, complete the fourth FAQ answer so it closes fully — readers and search crawlers both see the FAQ, and an unfinished answer undermines credibility on a piece that is otherwise tight and well-sourced.

Strong TOF execution overall: the H1 question format, 'Clear Verdict' section, answer-first structure under the first H2, and two internal links pointing down the funnel all meet spec cleanly. For your next TOF piece, consider whether the 'What Research Actually Shows' section title could be made more specific — something like 'What Visa's Pilot Data Actually Proves' would better signal topical authority and align more precisely with the long-tail SEO patterns in our H2 guidelines (Template A or B format).

INTERNAL LINKS ARE MISSING — THIS IS THE MOST URGENT FIX: As a MOF article, you must include a minimum of 2 internal markdown links: at least one pointing to a TOF article and one pointing to a BOF article. Per the SEO guidelines internal linking table, this article on AI fraud detection should link to 'fintech-ai-infrastructure-investment' using anchor text '80% of fintech firms going all-in on AI infrastructure' and to 'agentic-ai-regulatory-gap-fintech' using anchor text 'agentic AI regulatory gap in fintech'. Add these as natural inline links within the body — e.g., in the context paragraph where you discuss AI infrastructure investment or in the regulatory risk section.

H1 MUST CONTAIN A PRIMARY SEO KEYWORD FROM THE MASTER LIST: Your H1 ('What AI Fraud Detection ROI Actually Proves — and Where the Business Case Breaks Down') does not include any of the 8 primary target keywords from the SEO guidelines (e.g., 'machine learning financial services', 'AI in finance', 'artificial intelligence banking'). Rewrite the H1 to lead with a primary keyword per the SEO H1 formula — for example: 'Machine Learning Financial Services: What Visa's $40B Fraud ROI Actually Proves'. This is the single highest-leverage SEO fix you can make.

THE SOURCING AND SUBSTANCE ARE GENUINELY STRONG — PROTECT THAT CREDIBILITY WITH TIGHTER ATTRIBUTION ON THE JPMORGAN FIGURE: Your Visa and Mastercard citations are well-verified and the methodological critique (no control groups, self-reported data, period selection flattering results) is exactly the kind of analysis Particle Post readers need. However, the JPMorgan $1.5 billion figure is sourced via Modernize.io citing a LinkedIn post — not a primary source. For a claim of this magnitude, trace it to JPMorgan's own earnings call transcript, annual report, or an investor relations disclosure. A Bloomberg or Reuters report citing JPMorgan directly would also meet the standard. Modernize.io via LinkedIn does not.

BOF Go/No-Go section is missing — this is a mandatory BOF structural element and your title explicitly promises it. Add a clearly labeled '## Go/No-Go Checkpoint' section with explicit binary pass/fail criteria (e.g., 'If labeled transaction data spans fewer than 18 months → NO-GO. If compliance team has not signed off on explainability layer → NO-GO'). Readers at the bottom of the funnel need this decision scaffold — it's what separates a BOF guide from a MOF explainer.

The H1 must contain a primary SEO keyword per guidelines. Your current H1 ('How to Deploy AI Fraud Detection Without Derailing Customer Experience or Failing a Regulatory Audit') contains none of the 8 target keywords. Rewrite to match the H1 formula: try 'AI in Finance: How to Deploy Fraud Detection Without Failing a Regulatory Audit 2026' or 'Machine Learning Financial Services: AI Fraud Detection Deployment Playbook 2026' — keep it under 70 characters for full SERP display.

Verify the 90% AI adoption figure before next run — Feedzai's confirmed public statistics from the same survey are 87% citing data challenges and 64% rolling out AI in the past two years. The 90% figure does not appear in confirmed search results and should either be sourced to a specific page in the Feedzai report or replaced with one of the verified figures. A single unverifiable statistic in a well-sourced article is the kind of detail that erodes reader trust with your CFO/CTO audience.

MOF internal linking is your most urgent fix: MOF articles require links to TOF content, BOF content, AND at least one additional MOF piece. You have one link (to the FCA explainability article). Add at minimum one link using the anchor text 'agentic AI regulatory gap in fintech' pointing to /posts/agentic-ai-regulatory-gap-fintech/, and one more to 'AI agents in corporate payments infrastructure' pointing to /posts/ai-agents-corporate-payments-infrastructure-readiness/. Both are available in the SEO linking table and are directly topically relevant.

The FAQ section must appear in the article body, not only in YAML frontmatter. You have five strong, well-written Q+A pairs — they just need to be rendered under a '## Frequently Asked Questions' H2 with ### Q: / **A:** formatting so readers (and featured-snippet crawlers) can actually see them. Moving them from frontmatter to body is a copy-paste task, but it's mandatory when has_faq=true.

Your MOF 'friction scenarios' are currently pattern-descriptions ('the replace-and-reduce narrative') rather than named-company failure cases. For the next revision, anchor at least one friction scenario to a real named organization — even a generic example like 'a Fortune 500 manufacturer that activated Oracle Fusion agents on unconsolidated GL data and triggered cascading payment errors' is stronger than a thematic label. Named specificity is what separates Particle Post from trade blog content.
[WEEKLY AUDIT] Missing frontmatter: categories — found in 6/7 articles this week. Fix this pattern in future articles.
[WEEKLY AUDIT] has_faq=true but FAQ section missing — found in 4/7 articles this week. Fix this pattern in future articles.
[WEEKLY AUDIT] No internal links to other posts — found in 3/7 articles this week. Fix this pattern in future articles.
[WEEKLY AUDIT] Cover image uses picsum.photos placeholder — found in 1/7 articles this week. Fix this pattern in future articles.

The H1 — 'The AI Fraud Arms Race: What the Research Shows About Detection ROI — and Where It Breaks' — does not contain any of the eight designated primary keywords from the SEO guidelines. Per the SEO rules, every H1 must contain at least one primary keyword unmodified or in close variant. Revise to something like: 'AI Fraud Detection ROI: What the Research Shows — and Where It Breaks' or 'AI Risk Management Finance: The Fraud Arms Race Banks Can't Afford to Lose 2026'. This is the single highest-priority fix before the next submission.

Clean up the submission pipeline: the article arrived with both a YAML-fronted formatted version and a second '[RESTRUCTURED ARTICLE]' plain-text duplicate below the sources cut-off. The formatter should deliver exactly one version. Having two versions risks the wrong one going to publish and creates confusion in the CMS. Enforce a single clean output handoff from the formatter.

The stat-box visual elements are rendered as 'STAT: $12.5B | label | source' plain-text markers rather than the '{{< stat-box number=... >}}' shortcode syntax required for the CMS to render them as styled callouts. Strong editorial instinct on placement and frequency — both stat-boxes land at exactly the right moments in the narrative. Just convert the syntax before publication so readers see formatted callouts, not raw marker strings.

Source URL completeness: You cite KPMG, ChatFin, RITS Center, and Snowflake inline with specific statistics, but none of these appear in the Sources section URLs. Every inline citation must have a corresponding URL at the bottom of the article. If you cannot locate a live, verifiable URL for a statistic, reframe it as context or remove it. This is your highest-priority fix for the next run — a Bloomberg editor would send this back immediately for missing source links.

H1 character count: The restructured H1 ('Agentic AI in Finance Operations: What the Oracle and Zalos Deployments Actually Prove — and Where the ROI Math Breaks') runs approximately 100 characters — well above the 70-character SERP display maximum in the SEO guidelines. Trim to a tighter formula: 'Agentic AI Finance Operations: What Oracle and Zalos Actually Prove' (67 chars) retains the primary keyword and named entities within the display window.

Em dash discipline: The article uses em dashes as stylistic connectors in multiple consecutive sentences within single paragraphs (especially in the lede and the 'What This Means for Three Finance Functions' section). The editorial guidelines flag this explicitly as an AI-generation tell when used more than once per paragraph. Do a final pass before submission: replace em dashes with periods, commas, or restructured clauses. One per paragraph is the hard ceiling.

Em dash discipline is the clearest remaining AI-generation signal in this piece. You used em dashes as connectors in multiple sentences across several paragraphs — the editorial guidelines flag this explicitly as an AI tell when it appears more than once per paragraph. In your next draft, do a find-replace pass on every em dash and ask whether a period, a colon, or a comma serves the sentence better. Reserve the em dash for genuine parenthetical asides, used once per paragraph maximum.

The MOF 'what the evidence does NOT prove' requirement is structurally present in your 'What Neither Approach Has Proven at Scale' section, but it reads as a continuation of the vendor comparison rather than a standalone analytical checkpoint. For your next MOF article, make this a dedicated labeled section that explicitly names the claims vendors are making and then specifically rebuts or qualifies each one with sourced evidence — or an honest acknowledgment that no independent data yet exists. This signals editorial rigor to your executive reader.

The H1 does not contain a clean primary keyword match from the SEO target list. 'Agentic AI finance' is a Priority 4 target keyword — your H1 has 'agentic' and 'finance' separated by other words, which may not satisfy SERP matching. A revision like 'Agentic AI Finance: Oracle's 22 Fusion Apps vs. Zalos — Which Fits Your Stack' would bring it into compliance without sacrificing the comparison framing that makes this piece useful.

EM-DASH BAN: This is your most urgent fix. Em-dashes appear at least four times in the article body and FAQ section — e.g., 'agentic AI — covering autonomous exception handling...' and 'per Snowfox.ai — these must be resolved before go-live.' Every single em-dash must be replaced before the next submission. Use a comma, colon, semicolon, or parentheses instead. The ban is absolute and carries a 15-point penalty per instance. Run a find-and-replace for the '—' character before submitting any article.

H1 KEYWORD ALIGNMENT: Your restructured body H1 reads 'How to Deploy AI Agents for Accounts Payable: 4-Step Implementation Roadmap' but the primary target keyword is 'AI accounts payable automation.' Google indexes the body H1, not the frontmatter title. Align the two so the H1 in the article body matches the frontmatter title: 'AI Accounts Payable Automation: 7-Step Implementation Guide' works perfectly and follows the SEO H1 formula. Never let the body H1 and frontmatter title diverge.

BOF FUNNEL COMPLIANCE — STRONG EXECUTION: This article correctly includes numbered implementation steps (7 steps), names four specific failure scenarios, and has a clearly labeled go/no-go decision checkpoint section with four explicit criteria. The two internal links pointing to deeper content are present. The FAQ section has five well-sourced Q+A pairs. This is a well-structured BOF piece — the em-dash issue is the only meaningful barrier to a clean submission.
